sections.heading: Overview
sections.paragraph: June 2026 demonstrated significant momentum in targeted protein degradation technology, characterized by major strategic partnerships and regulatory milestones distinguishing this modality from traditional inhibition. Roche announced a substantial collaboration with Nurix Therapeutics to co-develop bexobrutideg, a Bruton’s tyrosine kinase (BTK) degrader, investing $700 million upfront to address resistance issues in blood cancers and autoimmune conditions. Concurrently, Amphista Therapeutics received FDA clearance for its IND application for AMX-883, a non-cereblon BRD9 degrader utilizing proprietary Targeted Glue™ technology, paving the way for Phase 1 trials in acute myeloid leukaemia. These developments underscore the industry's shift towards eliminating protein targets rather than merely inhibiting them. While broader pipeline landscapes faced mixed signals in other therapeutic areas, the momentum in protein degradation remains robust, with Roche and Amphista positioning their respective programs to overcome limitations of traditional small molecules and establish new treatment pathways for complex diseases. The convergence of these activities signals a robust expansion of the targeted protein degradation pipeline beyond early research into late-stage clinical validation and commercial positioning, reflecting high industry confidence in next-generation therapeutics capable of eliminating target proteins.

points.text: Roche — bexobrutideg — Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia/Non-Hodgkin Lymphoma — Collaboration with Nurix Therapeutics
Amphista Therapeutics — AMX-883 — Acute Myeloid Leukaemia — FDA IND Clearance
Roche: Strategic objective to leverage targeted protein degradation to overcome BTK inhibitor resistance in blood cancers and autoimmune conditions.
Amphista Therapeutics: Strategic objective to transition into a clinical-stage company using proprietary Eclipsys® platform for non-cereblon degraders.
Nurix Therapeutics: Strategic objective to partner with Roche for codevelopment of bexobrutideg to elevate competitiveness.
bexobrutideg: Mechanism degrades Bruton’s tyrosine kinase (BTK) to sidestep resistance; Phase 3 study in CLL expected to begin summer 2026.
AMX-883: Mechanism recruits DCAF16 to degrade BRD9 via Targeted Glue™ technology; Phase 1 monotherapy trial for relapsed/refractory AML and high-risk MDS.
Roche and Nurix Therapeutics: $700 million upfront payment with potential up to $2.3 billion; Roche covers 60% of development costs and splits U.S. rights.
